blocks.content: Thursday. Second to last day of October.
I was cutting through the park on the way home — the long way, because I needed the extra blocks. The air was cold in that specific way that means it means it now. Not pretending.
Diego was there. On a bench near the courts, backpack between his feet, watching the empty court like there was a game going on. He had one earbud in. The other dangled.
I sat down. He pulled the earbud out.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: You okay?

blocks.speaker: Diego
blocks.content: Yeah. Just didn't feel like going home yet.
He said it like it was nothing. Just a logistical decision. But I knew a little about what was at home.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: Your mom working tonight?

blocks.speaker: Diego
blocks.content: Yeah. Double.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: Your uncle?

blocks.speaker: Diego
blocks.content: Probably.
He didn't elaborate. I didn't push. We both understood what probably meant in that sentence.

blocks.speaker: Diego
blocks.content: You ever get tired of doing the math?

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: What math.

blocks.speaker: Diego
blocks.content: Every time you go somewhere. Every time you make a move. Is this safe, is that guy going to be a problem, which way do I walk home — all of it. The math. You ever just want to go somewhere where you don't have to run it every time?
He was looking at the empty court. Thirteen years old and tired in the bone way.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: Yeah. I want that.

blocks.speaker: Diego
blocks.content: I just want to walk somewhere without knowing who's on which corner first.

We sat until it got cold. A few kids came to the court eventually, started shooting around. Diego watched them for a while — not envious, just watching. Like he was trying to remember what that looked like from the outside.
We went our separate ways when it got dark. I walked home thinking about the math comment. About a thirteen-year-old sitting on a park bench because home had the wrong person in it.
I thought: he needs something to move toward. Everybody does.

blocks.prompt: Diego. Thirteen years old doing math he shouldn't have to do yet. What does Larry feel sitting there with him?

options.label: That he's going to be okay. He's smarter and more careful than the block deserves.
That being careful isn't always enough. And that's what scares him.
